channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: Mentor teachers: catalysts in empathy
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nivedita Bedadur

This article is the first in a series of articles on mentoring as a strategy for developing academic leaders. The series will explore models of teacher mentoring in India in state board schools, Kendriya Vidyalayas, private schools, and of school heads. The series showcases best practices in the field, some of them predating NCF 2020 and The Blue Book on Mentoring.*

A mentor is defined as a knowledgeable and experienced guide, a trusted ally and advocate, and a caring role model. (Blue Book on NMM page 2) A widely accepted modern view is that mentoring is a developmental relationship with relational and instrumental aspects. It is a two-way street, where both mentor and mentee learn and grow. Mentoring can be defined simply as sharing knowledge, skills, and experience in order to encourage and empower another person. In contexts of teacher development, this process involves enhancing teachers’ autonomy to develop for themselves, increasing their ability and willingness to take control of their own learning rather than judging or directly advising them, or giving them all the answers. (Smith, R 2020).

What is the National Mission on Mentoring for India?

The National Mission on Mentoring is a mission to develop a pool of experienced mentors at all levels of learning to share their expertise with teachers, from new inductees to experienced teachers. It envisages mentoring for personal and professional growth of both the mentor and the mentee, a process which capacitates the mentee in ‘on the ground’ problem solving, pedagogical innovation, and practical skill, creating empowered and confident school leaders.

What are the attributes of a mentor?

A mentor must be an experienced professional. She must have both theoretical and practical knowledge as well as empathy. The most important skills that a mentor needs to develop are active listening, creating safe sharing spaces, and critical questioning. A mentor needs to empower by refraining from offering solutions, but help mentees arrive at them by engaging in reflective practice.

Why is mentoring so important?

Mentoring has been looked at as a possible solution to the policy-practice puzzle that Indian education is plagued with. We are all aware that government policies do not translate into implementation on the ground due to several reasons. The Indian education system is one of the largest in the world with nearly 14.71 lakh schools, more than 1.01 crore teachers and 24.69 crore students from diverse socio-economic backgrounds and contexts. (UDISE plus.gov.in 2024-25). The alignment between the government and different educational institutions at the state level, the contexts and cultures of different territories in India, the translation of policies into doables are some of the roadblocks in implementation. In this article, we will examine the Delhi Government Mentor Teacher (MT) model, which tried to overcome the difficulties of implementing a government policy to improve learning levels in public schools in Delhi.

Chunauti and Buniyad^ – Delihi’s Mentor Teacher Model of Education Reform: where policy translated into practice

There are 1534 MCD (Municipal Corporation of Delhi) schools in Delhi, 850,000 students, and 19000 teachers. Almost 80 percent of the students belong to low socio-economic status and around 50 percent are first generation learners (Kaushik, 2024). In 2016, the Delhi government took up the challenge of improving the learning levels of 1.6 million students in MCD schools by mentoring more than 1000 secondary teachers with the help of about 200 Mentor Teachers (MTs) and Teacher Development Coordinators (TDCs). A massive recruitment drive was conducted to attract teacher leaders to apply for the mentor teacher post. Mentors were selected after a stringent training process and were trained in leadership skills, innovative pedagogies, and mentoring. A deep dive immersion in best practices was organized through visits to different education institutes in India. To share the load of mentoring teachers and achieve the goal of developing teacher resource groups at the school level, TDCs were appointed in 2017.

What were the roles and responsibilities of mentor teachers and teacher development coordinators?

Each MT was in charge of four to five schools. They reported at the district level and did not take up actual classroom work. They visited one school every day, held teacher meetings, met with the head teacher, gave on-site support to teachers through classroom observations, assisted in lesson planning, and held teacher development workshops. They generated reports based on their visits and liasoned with the education department. The role of the MT extended beyond the classroom, they acted as friends, guides, and facilitators for teachers. They were involved in a massive movement of curriculum reform and content development, lesson planning, and innovative practices.

The TDCs were stationed in schools. They observed classes daily, identified the learning needs of students, provided feedback to address academic challenges and developed an academic resource group at the school level.

Both these posts were at the same level and individuals were trained to develop a symbiotic relationship with teachers.

The MTs and the TDCs were primarily responsible for liaisoning between the government and schools to translate policies on ground. Their role was to discuss challenges on the ground with the higher authorities. To facilitate this, a weekly cadence was set up at the zonal level, monthly cadence at the district level, and bi monthly cadence at the state level. This alignment was ensured for two-way communication.

The model of mentorship adopted by the Delhi government very specifically discouraged monitoring. The MTs and TDCs were expected to face and work through challenges. Top-down instructions and lecturing was not encouraged. The academic resource group learnt through sharing and reflection and discussion and research.

The challenges of catalysing change

Resistance to labelling students

The MTs and TDCs faced many challenges. The initial challenge of building relationships with heads of schools and teachers was overcome with the appointment of TDCs at the school level. But as innovations evolved and other players, experts from the field got involved, the challenges grew. The government adopted the TaRL philosophy of teaching at the right level and grouped children according to their learning levels into three groups Neo Nishtha: dropouts and failing class 9 more than once, Nishtha: students scoring less than 33%, and Pratibha: students scoring more than 33%. The teachers conveyed their strong objection to this because they felt it was like labelling students. Master trainers and TDCs helped teachers understand the intention behind the grouping, which was flexible and not fixed. Parents and children too were unhappy about the grouping. The MTs and TDCs were instrumental in creating an understanding amongst the stakeholders.

Empowering teachers: I’m the captain of my ship

The first set of materials for Teaching at the Right level were prepared at the district level. Teachers were unhappy with the materials. They expressed their concerns vocally. The MTs carried these voices to the highest level asserting that no one knew their students, their context, and their needs better than the teachers. A massive subject-wise summer workshop was held at the district level, in which the teachers with the help of MTs and TDCs brought out the celebrated Pragati series of resource material.

Personality matters: from falling to flying

The MTs faced a variety of challenges. They became the guardian angels of their respective schools. When the sorting of students happened, the attendance of Neo Nishthas dropped alarmingly. Every MT and TDC tried different methods of enhancing student attendance. Two MTs shared innovative practices of increasing student attendance. One MT organized free health camps for families and grabbed the opportunity of convincing parents to send their children to school. Another used the loud speaker of a nearby mosque to remind parents to send their children to school.

Conclusion

A teacher says, “Last five or six years, there has been a paradigm shift in our schools. Now the teachers have developed the practice of discussing academic issues, issues related to the classroom” (Thakur & Gupta, 2025). The Delhi Government Mentor Teacher Model did not guarantee success. There were many ups and downs along the way. Yet, this model changed the grammar of teacher development by providing support and then insisting on improved accountability. It empowered a simple classroom teacher to lead a group by widening her horizon, enhancing her knowledge base, and encouraging innovation.

References

  1. Aiyar, Y., Davis, V., Govindan, G., & Kapoor, T. (2021). *Rewriting the grammar of education systems: Delhi’s education reform (A tale of creative resistance and creative disruption).* Research on Improving Systems of Education (RISE).
  2. Batra, P. (2009). Teacher education in India: Theoretical and policy perspectives. *Contemporary Education Dialogue, 6*(2), 121–156.
  3. Boston Consulting Group. (n.d.). *School education reforms in Delhi 2015–2020: Interventions handbook.* https://web-assets.bcg.com
  4. Childress, D. (2021). *Supporting teachers from the middle tier: The Teacher Development Coordinator Program in Delhi.* International Institute for Educational Planning & UNESCO.
  5. Gavari, A. (2019). *Delhi revolution (The Pragati Series).* Azim Premji Foundation. https://publications.azimpremjifoundation.org
  6. Joshi, M. (2019, October 21). Mentor teacher – Delhi government’s sauce for education reforms. *The Indian Express.*
  7. Kaushik, M., & Chaudhary, S. (2024). *A case study from Delhi Project Management Unit.* Vibha vibha.org
  8. State Council of Education Research and Training. (n.d.). *SCERT Delhi.* https://scert.delhi.gov.in
  9. Shanthakumar, V., Khandpur, N. K. D., & Jain, S. (2018). *Political change and education reforms: Lessons from Delhi under Aam Aadmi Party.* Azim Premji University.
  10. Thakur, T., & Gupta, Y. (2025). An evaluation of the mentor teacher program in Delhi government schools. *National Journal of Education, XXIII*(10).
  11. Yadav, S., & Kanvaria, V. K. (2019). *Towards transformative science pedagogy: Critical insights from government schools of NCT.* Vibha. https://vibha.org

*The Blue Book on Mentoring is a book of guidelines by the Government of India brought out by the National Council for Teacher Education on March 9 2024 to draw out the road map for the National Mission on Mentoring.

^The Chunauti Program 2016 to 2017, Buniyad 2017 to 2023.

Nivedita Bedadur is happiest when she is with children and teachers, learning, unlearning, and teaching. Over years of mentoring and leading teacher capacity development, she has refined her listening and relearning muscle. Presently, she is researching for her upcoming book on the lives of the marginalized people of Konkan. She can be reached at nitavbedadur@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nivedita Bedadur
What happens when mentoring replaces monitoring? Nivedita Bedadur writes about how empathy-driven teacher mentorship translated education policy into classroom practice in Delhi.

item.title: Each One Reach One: empathy in action
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nivedita V. Bedadur

“They asked themselves just one question! What if we reach out to just one child each, support just one child at a time. Can we change our world? And thus was born an initiative, a campaign, a journey towards empowerment for children who had been left behind for one reason or other.”[1]

What happens when a teacher mentors a child, builds a relationship, and supports them emotionally? Deputy Commissioner Regional Office, Guwahati Region, Assam, Shri Chandrashekhar Azad, recollects…

“Today a class 12 student who was once suicidal and had said that she felt like giving up in life because she failed in class 9 is sitting on the front bench, eagerly listening to her teacher and participating in class activities. Looking back she tells me how her teacher mentored her through the difficult days. The teacher says, ‘I did not do much, I just held her hand when she needed it.’”

It seems impossible to scale up such a one-on-one mentoring programme for students to an entire region, marked by difficult terrain and diverse microclimates. Yet, the teachers, principals, and even miscellaneous staff of the 42 schools of Kendriya Vidyalayas of the Guwahati Region of Assam made it happen.

Kendriya Vidyalayas are schools established by the Human Resource Ministry to provide uninterrupted education to the children of transferable government employees and defence personnel. The vision of Kendriya Vidyalayas is to foster a spirit of national integration, innovation, and experimentation. The Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan is committed to improving the quality of education in line with NEP 2020, through innovative practices in its 25 regions. Each one Reach One is one such inclusive and innovative initiative.

Each hand extended is a life transformed…

It all began when the Deputy Commissioner, Shri Chandrashekhar Azad, spoke to the children of class 12 about their feelings and fears related to studies and examinations. In a shocking revelation, the anonymous chits spoke about anxiety, depression, fear of examination and of certain subjects, and even emotional breakdown. Many children had written about disappointing their parents and teachers. Visibly moved, the Deputy Commissioner appealed to the teachers with an inclusive and innovative plan, Each One Reach One. This was not a policy or an official order, it was simply an appeal from one heart to another. Yet, it was a massive plan, with a strong purpose, to reach out to the 42 schools of the region. The Guwahati Region of Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan comprises all the Kendriya Vidyalayas in Assam. A simple appeal, not an official order, and yet teachers, headmasters, principals, Assistant Commissioners, and even the administrative staff responded overwhelmingly. They pledged to mentor and support one child each, who had been identified with emotional, conceptual, or academic issues and challenges. It was a long-term engagement for the entire year of 2024-25. Although each mentor only supported one child, it was an arduous task as it involved home visits and engagement with the child beyond school hours, emotional investment, and a responsibility to bring joy and nurture the child.

KYC: Know Your Child – a simple but profound mantra

Her mentor teacher had almost given up on the mentee, she just did not seem interested in studies. Such a challenge was a common experience. Then, in a mentor meeting, the Deputy Commissioner suggested visiting the child’s home. It was discovered that the parents had not given an address and nothing was known about the child’s home. The child too was reluctant to share it. After many enquiries, through a friend, the teacher visited the child’s home and was shocked to discover that her parents were no more and that she was living with her aunt, who was not very welcoming. Obviously, the child had no home support. Realizing this, the mentor was deeply moved and their interactions became richer. Teachers soon began to see the truth of the Deputy Commissioner's one and only principle of mentoring success, KYC, i.e., Know Your Child. A glance at the common support schedule reveals the depth of thought behind the KYC. It also highlights the coordination of different teachers who support the mentor.

[2]

Children in the Guwahati Region are supported by being heard, recognized, and valued. They are also helped to develop conceptual clarity. As each child with special needs was paired with a mentor, it became evident that much more needs to be done. Plans had to be tailor-made for the academic progress of each child, worksheets and interactive teaching learning materials had to be planned to bridge gaps, wellness and mindfulness activities had to be designed. Self-affirmation activities had to be designed, parents had to be taken into confidence and after-school visits had to be planned. It was a massive task, an onerous responsibility for every member indeed. Yet, this voluntary task was done with a smile and the satisfaction of being the light in a child’s eyes!

“Behind every child in need is a story!”

While the impact of the appeal spread wide and deep, mentors undertook a need analysis of children from Balvatika to grade 12. The need analysis revealed that out of a total strength of 41, 541 students from 42 schools, 2026 students needed help. Amongst them, 12.6% had attention related challenges, 9.01% had emotional challenges, 21.7% students struggled with STEM subjects, and 29.4% faced literacy difficulties. Child and mentor were paired on the basis of voluntary adoption and skill areas. The mentor then did several rounds of mentor-mentee meet and greet to get to know the child. The mentor engaged with the parents and class teacher of the child to understand the child’s challenges. A tentative plan was then made in agreement with the child and parents. Often, the mentor visited the child at home and engaged with them beyond school hours. Careful records were maintained using templates designed by the regional office. Regular meetings were organized and the Deputy Commissioner himself motivated the mentors.

“I had almost given up on myself after failing in two subjects. But my mentor never gave up on me. I started trying again because my mentor believed I could,” says a class 7 student. Most students faced challenges at home – broken families, conflicts, and illness. As mentors shared their experiences with each other, one of them remarked, “It is only after becoming a mentor that I realized that every disturbed or non-performing child has a story to tell, which will touch your heart.”[3]

From challenges to confidence

This mission impacted more than 2000 students out of the 41,541 students in the region, rekindling hope, self-esteem and a love of learning in them. Unlike a large scale one-size-fits-all initiative, this programme engaged the human factor, while demonstrating sustainability and scalability. The impact of the mission reached and included almost five percent of the students with challenges. The regional office of Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan at Guwahati organized large scale training for mentors in wellness activities as well as SMART (Specific Measurable Achievable, Relevant, and Timely) tools for measuring the impact of all three domains: psychomotor, cognitive, and emotional. This empowered group of mentors helped their mentees set small goals and celebrate their successes. They showed them the path to bridge their learning gaps, supported them in building communication skills and emotional reassurance. This led to 99.9% pass rate in classes 9 to 12.

Looking back and forward…

This programme completed one year in May 2025. Yet, it is not over, it continues with the same spirit in the Guwahati Region as well as the other regions of Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan. The Commissioner of KVS has recommended that all regions should plan to take up this initiative. This is because Each One Reach One is not just a campaign, but a heartfelt commitment to inclusivity, compassion, and community building.

References

  1. (2025) Assam Teachers combat student anxiety and academic stress through ‘Each One Reach One’. KRC Times. krctimes.com.
  2. (2025) A Story of healing and holistic growth, neindiabroadcast.com July 28.
  3. (2025) Beyond the blackboard: Assam Teachers turn mentors to help students tide over anxiety disorders, education.com July 28.
  4. Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan dsel.education.gov.in.
  5. (2025) Each One Reach One: The KVS mentoring changing lives education economic times.indiatimes.com.
  6. (2025) Each One Reach One: Transforming students and lives through mentorship.
  7. NEP 2020 Success Stories, Ministry of Education @EduMin of India. X.EduMin Of India.
  8. (2025) One person can change another person’s world. https: //cdnbbsr.s3was.gov.in.

[1] The author would like to acknowledge with gratitude the inputs given in a telephone conversation by the Deputy Commissioner of Guwahati Region in this article. The author also acknowledges inputs from the material released by the regional office of the Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan on its social media accounts.

[2] This table has been adapted from the KVS RO Guwahati Region PPT titled Each One Reach One, which is in the public domain.

[3] Adapted from TGT English KV Guwahati Region as quoted in the social media.

Nivedita V. Bedadur is happiest when she is with children and teachers, learning, unlearning, and teaching. Over years of mentoring and leading teacher capacity development, she has refined her listening and relearning muscle. Presently, she is researching for her upcoming book on the lives of the marginalized people of Konkan. She can be reached at nitavbedadur@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nivedita V. Bedadur
Can one teacher change one life? Nivedita V. Bedadur writes about educators across 42 Guwahati schools who proved this was possible.

item.title: Saksham Haryana: empowering mentorship
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nivedita V Bedadur

The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 proposes to overhaul the education system in alignment with Sustainable Development Goal 4 (SDG4): Quality Education. It recognizes that teacher professional development requires a multidisciplinary perspective, cultivation of professional dispositions, ethical values, and continuous enhancement of teaching-learning practices through structured mentoring by experienced and competent mentors. (web.ncte.gov.in)

This series has so far examined large scale mentorship programmes conducted by different entities: state governments and Kendriya Vidyalayas, both fine examples of models of mentoring that provide conceptual clarity, emotional support, and empowerment to the child. This article brings you yet another such programme predating even The National Mission on Mentoring (NMM), a programme that is more of a revolution, where along with the mentors and mentees, other stakeholders also came together to ensure its success.

From 2014 to 2025, Haryana has implemented Saksham – a successful mentorship programme – to improve student outcomes. ASER 2012 had come as a shock to Haryana. ASER results for Haryana before 2012 showed that 67% students studying in grade V could comprehend grade 1 material compared to the national average of 54%. There was also progress in student enrollment at that time.

In 2012, however, student enrollment dropped to 1.5%. ASER results also showed that only 25.3% of children in grade 3 could read simple English sentences, 34.1% of third graders could read texts meant for grade 2, and only 46.1% could do simple basic subtraction. In 2014, only 40% students could do grade appropriate reading and subtraction. The report also showed that there was a growing disparity between children enrolled in government schools and those in private schools.

Saksham Haryana

Haryana has 22 districts, 20 lakh students in government schools and 11 lakh educators. The Saksham Haryana Programme began in 2013 in a small way with the strengthening of the roles of key actors and freeing them from the administrative burden of being the government’s assistants, so that they could concentrate on academics alone. Block Resource Persons (BRPs) and Assistant Block Resource Coordinators (ABRCs) became key figures in the Saksham revolution, acting as mentors to primary teachers. They were the critical actors in developing training and pedagogical support for teachers.

Roles and responsibilities of BRPs and ABRCs

BRPs helped with pedagogical aspects, visited schools frequently, supervised the progression of curricula, planned and coordinated training programmes and disseminated best practices. ABRCs were responsible for a certain number of cluster schools in a block. They provided academic support, gathered data, and prepared reports based on the data. They were also the link between the government and the last mile, escalating issues and needs at proper levels.

Mentors visited schools, observed classes, teacher performance and student interaction, and provided feedback. They guided teachers in their professional goals, long-term commitments, and planned future meetings as necessary. Mentors also updated the dashboard of the Samiksha assessment app and conducted on-the-spot assessment of students to check their competency levels.

Saksham 1.0

The Government of Haryana partnered with Samagra, a private firm for training mentors. The training happened in a cascade mode and began with an introduction to the framework of Cooperation, Feedback, and Diligence. Cooperation included assessing classes and supporting teachers; Feedback included nurturing positive teacher attitudes using constructive ways of enhancing performance; and Diligence included regular school visits and follow ups. In the classroom, mentors played the role of advisors, auditors, and mediators.

Learning from past mistakes, the non-government partners of Saksham Haryana strongly felt that certain key enablers had to be put in place to empower both mentors and mentees if they had to attain the ambitious goal of 80% students achieving grade-level competencies in all public schools and districts of Haryana.

In 2017, with the launch of Saksham 1.0, several key enablers were put in place to empower both mentors and mentees.

The Saksham Chakra


Saksham Chakra is the journey of Haryana’s 15000 schools, one lakh teachers, 1300 mentors and 20,00,000 students to attain 80% achievement of grade-level competencies. This required a systemic education transformation. There were four enablers that were planned and executed.

Administrative enablers

Administrative enablers included the following questions:

  1. Are there enough teachers in state board schools to take up this responsibility? It was discovered that there were 25% vacancies. By the end of the term, 7000 teachers were on-boarded.
  2. Is there equitable distribution of teachers in schools? The answer to this was ‘No’. A rule-based online teacher transfer policy was designed and teacher transfer application and process became transparent and online.
  3. Does instructional material reach schools on time? The answer was ‘not always’. Provision was made for streamlining the delivery of textbooks.
  4. How much time do teachers spend doing administrative duties? The answer was more than 50% of their time. To change this, administrative duties like maintaining service books, were digitized and all HR processes were moved online.

Academic enablers

Academic intervention from rote to competency-based learning required improvement in both people and processes. A Saksham Talika (list of competencies) was prepared and distributed to teachers. Teacher awareness of these competencies was enhanced through workshops. Teachers prepared their own Saksham Talika and maintained personal student records. About 1300 mentors were on-boarded. Each mentor visited 10 schools every month and conducted training and remedial workshops and gave support where needed. A group of resource teachers from every district created pedagogical materials and disseminated them.

Assessment Reforms

A bimonthly test was conducted for the students to test their learning and application of concepts. Mentor dashboards were designed for record maintenance. Teachers and mentors were trained to use and upload records to the dashboard.

Participation of the CMO

To galvanize the system, certain state level measures were also taken.

CMO involvement: To move from goal to action, the CM himself made several announcements. A Saksham Cell was set up in the CMO office. Csacaded bi-monthly reviews were conducted by the CMO. Reviews were conducted regularly at block, cluster, and district levels. Progress was mapped and accountability was fixed at each level. Dissemination of information, pedagogical support, and listing blocks that achieved their target was done regularly through WhatsApp groups and government issued circulars.

Saksham Ghoshana: Saksham Ghoshana gamified the assessment of the programme, as each block could raise their hands when they were ready for the external assessment of their achievement of 80% competency at grade level. The external assessment would then test them and declare the block Saksham. The results were incentivized and widely distributed. Ten rounds of Saksham assessment were conducted for Hindi and maths. Saksham + was conducted for Hindi, maths, and English. By 2019 (before COVID-19 struck) 86% of the students across the state had achieved grade-level competencies as compared to 50% in 2016. Saksham 1.0 was implemented between 2017 and 2019. In the March 2019 Ghoshana, 94 blocks or 80 % of all blocks achieved grade-level competencies in Hindi and maths for grades 3, 5, and 7. A state level felicitation ceremony was conducted and all the blocks that achieved their targets, outstanding students, their mentors, and teachers were awarded.

Saksham 2.0, which was carried out from 2021 to 2025, extended this concept to include environmental science and the social sciences. There were two goals: institutionalization of best practices and inclusion of secondary grades and subjects in the programme. More mentors were recruited with focus on the secondary grades and scholarships to deserving students. But COVID-19 threw a curved ball and the entire focus shifted to developing education technology. As the lockdown eased, Saksham Samiksha app now recorded temperature along with daily attendance. Pre-loaded tablets or digital tabs were distributed to students. Parents enrolled themselves as support for their children and were on-boarded into the programme.

A mentor reports, “Before Saksham we did a lot of trainings but the achievements were negligible; today the focus on achievement of competencies, the enablers like teacher recruitment and dissemination of best practices have motivated us and the gamification of Saksham Ghosna has empowered us through recognition at the national level. The success of the programme lies in the orchestrated efforts of the CMO, teachers, we mentors, students, and non-state actors like Samagra.”

Conclusion

Globally, policies are seen as an agreed framework for action, but in the case of India, implementation of policies fails on the ground because of lack of funds, political will, and commitment. The Haryana government’s Saksham mentorship programme succeeded in achieving its targets in record time because road blocks were removed and achievers were empowered, apart from the fact that there was political will and commitment.

References

  • Atthavar, S., Kothuri, A., DeBacker, H., Shailesh, A. S., Lima, L., Ohyama, K., Mangla, A., & Tudor, M. (2024). Teacher mentorship in India: Improving pedagogy in government schools. Reach Alliance. https://reachalliance.org
  • Sharan, A., Sanghvi, C., Kaladi, M., & Jaswani, P. (2017). Best practices and their role in policy implementation: Case of Saksham Haryana. Indian Political Economy (IPE). https://ipeindia.org
  • The Better India. (2006, February 6). Haryana holds blueprint for improving government schools across India: We tell you why. https://www.thebetterindia.com
  • The Times of India. (2018, May 10). 350 government schools in Haryana get Saksham status. https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com
  • National Council for Teacher Education. (n.d.). Official website. https://web.ncte.govt.in
  • Saksham Haryana: The educational transformation [Explainer video]. (n.d.). YouTube.

Nivedita V Bedadur is happiest when she is with children and teachers, learning, unlearning, and teaching. Over years of mentoring and leading teacher capacity development, she has refined her listening and relearning muscle. Presently, she is researching for her upcoming book on the lives of the marginalized people of Konkan. She can be reached at nitavbedadur@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nivedita V Bedadur
Can political will truly transform public education? Saksham Haryana suggests it can. Launched to reverse falling learning levels in government schools, the initiative replaced rote memorization with competency-based learning, empowered mentors to support teachers, and reduced bureaucratic burdens through smart reforms. With gamified assessments sparking district-wide competition and adaptive strategies during COVID-19, the program delivered measurable gains in student performance. How did one state turn around its education system? The full story reveals a powerful blueprint for change.
